reviews.author: Naty
reviews.text: It's good to know and remember the past..USA always so bad...

reviews.author: Danny Hyun
reviews.text: The origin of the Cave of Eight Women is related to the Second Indochina War. On November 14, 1972, US forces dropped a bomb on 20 Quyet Thang Street in Quang Binh Province. At the time, eight young female volunteers who were filling a bomb crater in the area had to run into a cave to avoid danger. Due to the huge explosion, a huge rock rolled and blocked the entrance of the cave, trapping 8 people inside the cave. Over the next few days, they were supplied with water and food through tubes, while the military outside worked to rescue them. However, by the ninth day, all were presumed dead with no survivors. To commemorate the sacrifice of the eight women, a shrine was built next to the cave, and it became known as the Cave of the Eight Women.

reviews.author: cryptomerioides T.
reviews.text: Eight Ladies Cave (Hang Tam Co) It was a fire flame day on November 14, 1972, when the US aircrafts dropped bombs on Victory Road 20. In order to avoid the destruction, a volunteers group ran into the nearby rock cave named Eight Ladies Cave. A series of bombs killed 5 artillery fighters. Soon after, a colossal rock fell and completely closed off the only mouth of cave. Eight young volunteers, four men and four women were tragically sealed inside the cave. All great efforts to rescue them were in vain...

reviews.author: Vincent Oliver
reviews.text: Very scenic drive to the site: two areas1) the offices, parking area, memorial wall and a area for seeing a story on TV ( no one around at our visit)- brief story of the red board - photo below. 2) 50m walk further up the road is the cave site, now a shallow overhang with a worship area ( sign not to enter) and the names of the 8 youth who died in the cave rock fall when it was bombed in 1972. Next to the cave is a shrine for prayers to these martyrs.

reviews.author: Megaworld Asia
reviews.text: This memorial site is on Route DT562 and just 2.5 km from the crossroads bridge. It is a Pagoda and a commemmoration site for eight local ladies who died in the cave, during the second Indo -China war. The site is quiet and serene and is worth a few minutes of contempation of the tragic events which occurred here some sixty years previously. Safe travels, Mega.

reviews.author: Min Ng
reviews.text: This is the sacred place one should pay respect for the fallen comrades sacrifice their life for the great cause

reviews.author: Great Places
reviews.text: It's a memorial cave and temple to the victims of the american/vietnam war. You can't go in to the cave. Only look in it to an alter

reviews.author: Devon Davis
reviews.text: The people posting negative comments here are unfortunately uneducated enough to understand what they are viewing. This is a beautiful memorial even as a foreigner I felt honored to show respects to the people who were martyrs and ultimately lost their lives helping and serving for their communities.

reviews.author: Lee
reviews.text: What you hear here will once again show you the strength of the Vietnamese people when the country needs it. So many people have devoted their youth and heroically sacrificed so that the country can be independent and happy.

reviews.author: Michael Le
reviews.text: It's ok if you don't know anywhere to go.

reviews.author: Emma Mccormick
reviews.text: Really interesting to learn about the history of this cave and how it played a part in the Vietnam war. This is a really important pilgrimage site for Vietnamese people due to the temple on site. The accessible part of the cave is quite small, so it’s more about the story and the history than the cave itself

reviews.author: Michelle Travas
reviews.text: The story of the 8 ladies is heart warming. It is a very clean place and it is really amazing how the locals of Vietnam respect their ancestors for all the sacrifice that was given during the war. Being there gives you peace of mind!

reviews.author: Seg Son Nga
reviews.text: Off the beaten path along the Ho Chi Minh West Trail where 8 volunteers were entombed in a cave by a boulder caused by US bombings in 1972. This site is in close vicinity of the various caves in the Phong Nha region

reviews.author: Cameron
reviews.text: It is a memorial, not a cave. We'll worth reading about the story behind it. Very tragic.

reviews.author: Łukasz Jędrys
reviews.text: 8 Ladies Cave. Place with amazing history from the Vietnam War. Tour Guide is recommended to get know details about this (and other in Phong Nha area) places and their connections to the war.

reviews.author: Charlie Schacher
reviews.text: Really interesting temple. Built to commemorate those who died constructing highway 20 during the Vietnam war.

reviews.author: Cam Harper
reviews.text: Pretty little spot and temple with tremendous significance for the Vietnamese, but this might be lost on most other visitors.

reviews.author: Trung Lê Quang
reviews.text: Wish you all 8 people, 4 women and 4 men Rest In Peace!!!

reviews.author: leo tran
reviews.text: Nice plce & peace with a lot of history story.

reviews.author: Jimmy Tran
reviews.text: The men who runs this place takes bribes in the form of goods.

reviews.author: Brandon
reviews.text: Cool history but not much to see

reviews.author: Tania Tomaselli
reviews.text: A bit disappointed, nothing to see but some inscription on a wall.

reviews.author: Graham Tebb
reviews.text: Very moving and in beautiful surroundings
